prompt: CHP project economics often depend on the spark spread, which is:

options.text: The difference between electricity purchase price and fuel cost on an equivalent energy basis
The gap between two motor nameplates only
Stack height divided by generator kW
Lighting footcandle levels in the plant

explanation: Favorable spark spread means onsite generation fuel cost is below avoided electricity cost.

distractorExplanations.b: Spark spread is an energy price relationship.
distractorExplanations.c: Physical dimensions are unrelated.
distractorExplanations.d: Lighting levels do not define spark spread.

prompt: Absorption chillers using waste heat from CHP are advantageous when:

options.text: There is sufficient thermal input and concurrent cooling load to utilize recovered heat
There is no cooling load and excess heat must be vented always
Electricity is cheaper than heat on all hours
Waste heat temperature is below ambient always

explanation: Heat-driven cooling leverages CHP thermal output, improving overall system efficiency.

distractorExplanations.b: Cooling load must exist to use absorption cooling.
distractorExplanations.c: Economics depend on relative energy prices.
distractorExplanations.d: Absorption requires adequate heat source temperature.

prompt: Industrial pumping systems often waste energy through:

options.text: Throttled valves, oversized pumps, and poor maintenance of impellers and seals
Perfect impeller trim matching all loads always without review
Elimination of all VFD options by code
Zero friction in pipes by default

explanation: System curve mismatches and control by throttling increase head loss and kWh.

distractorExplanations.b: Many plants have throttling or oversizing issues.
distractorExplanations.c: VFDs are commonly applied to save energy.
distractorExplanations.d: Friction always exists in real piping.

prompt: Applying VFDs to rotary screw compressors can save energy when:

options.text: Demand varies and inlet modulation or unload modes would otherwise waste power
Demand is constant at full load 100% of hours
Compressors never operate at part load
Leak rates are zero regardless of pressure

explanation: VFDs or efficient control modes reduce power at part load compared to constant speed with blow-off.

distractorExplanations.b: Constant full load offers less VFD benefit.
distractorExplanations.c: Part load is common in many plants.
distractorExplanations.d: Leaks affect system load.

prompt: Pinch analysis in industrial heat integration identifies:

options.text: The minimum temperature approach limiting heat recovery between hot and cold streams
Only electrical power factor correction needs
Roof reflectance values exclusively
BACnet object naming conventions

explanation: Pinch temperature helps maximize recovery with heat exchanger networks.

distractorExplanations.b: Pinch is thermal integration, not power factor.
distractorExplanations.c: Roof metrics are envelope topics.
distractorExplanations.d: BACnet naming is controls-related.

prompt: Solar PV module degradation over time typically:

options.text: Reduces output gradually due to cell aging, soiling, and potential-induced degradation
Increases wattage above STC rating every year
Eliminates all inverter losses immediately
Has no effect on capacity factor

explanation: Financial models use degradation rates (often ~0.5%/year) for long-term production estimates.

distractorExplanations.b: Output declines, not increases.
distractorExplanations.c: Inverter losses remain.
distractorExplanations.d: Degradation lowers capacity factor over time.

prompt: Inverter clipping occurs when:

options.text: DC array maximum power exceeds inverter AC rating and output is limited at peak sun
The inverter exceeds nameplate AC output indefinitely
Modules produce no power at standard test conditions
Battery state of charge is irrelevant to PV output

explanation: Oversizing DC to AC ratio trades occasional clipping for higher annual yield per inverter cost.

distractorExplanations.b: Inverters limit output at rated AC capacity.
distractorExplanations.c: Modules produce power at STC by definition.
distractorExplanations.d: Batteries are separate from PV clipping though hybrid systems interact.

prompt: Ground-source heat pump COP improves when:

options.text: Entering source temperature is favorable relative to required lift (heating or cooling mode)
Ground loop is completely dry with no fluid
Condenser approach is ignored in design
Compressors run with failed refrigerant charge

explanation: Stable ground temperatures reduce lift compared to air-source extremes, improving COP.

distractorExplanations.b: Closed loops require heat transfer fluid.
distractorExplanations.c: Approach affects heat exchanger performance.
distractorExplanations.d: Proper charge is required for COP.

prompt: Biogas from anaerobic digesters can fuel boilers or CHP if:

options.text: Gas is cleaned to meet equipment fuel specifications and heating value is sufficient
Raw digester gas requires no treatment ever
Methane content is irrelevant to combustion
All sulfur compounds are beneficial for burners

explanation: H2S, moisture, and siloxanes must be managed to protect combustion equipment.

distractorExplanations.b: Treatment is often required.
distractorExplanations.c: Methane content drives heating value.
distractorExplanations.d: Sulfur compounds are corrosive and harmful.

prompt: Steam header pressure management in a plant should:

options.text: Match the lowest pressure adequate for all connected loads to minimize losses
Always operate at maximum boiler pressure regardless of end use
Eliminate all pressure reducing valves
Ignore diversity of load when setting pressure

explanation: Excess pressure increases losses and trap wear; cascading pressures can optimize distribution.

distractorExplanations.b: Maximum pressure increases losses if not needed.
distractorExplanations.c: PRVs are used to step down to use points.
distractorExplanations.d: Load diversity affects optimal header pressure.

prompt: Boiler cycling losses increase when:

options.text: The boiler frequently short-cycles at low load without turndown or buffer capacity
The boiler runs steadily at matched load
Oxygen trim maintains stable combustion
Thermal mass or staging accommodates load swings

explanation: Short cycling purges heat and increases standby losses, reducing seasonal efficiency.

distractorExplanations.b: Steady operation reduces cycling losses.
distractorExplanations.c: Trim supports stable combustion.
distractorExplanations.d: Buffer/storage reduces cycling frequency.

prompt: Industrial boiler load profiling helps energy managers:

options.text: Identify opportunities for scheduling, staging, and right-sizing equipment
Ignore all time-of-use rate structures
Eliminate need for steam meters
Assume constant load 8760 hours

explanation: Load profiles reveal peak demand, part-load hours, and shutdown opportunities.

distractorExplanations.b: Time-of-use affects cost optimization.
distractorExplanations.c: Meters support profiling.
distractorExplanations.d: Loads vary in real facilities.

prompt: A Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) for onsite solar typically means:

options.text: A third party owns and operates the system while the site buys energy at agreed rates
The site must always own modules and inverters outright with no contracts
Utility rates become irrelevant to project economics
No production monitoring is required

explanation: PPAs transfer capital and O&M to developers in exchange for energy payments.

distractorExplanations.b: Third-party ownership is common in PPAs.
distractorExplanations.c: Utility rates often set PPA competitiveness.
distractorExplanations.d: Monitoring verifies production and billing.

prompt: When calculating emissions from distributed generation, energy managers should account for:

options.text: Fuel carbon content, generation efficiency, and displaced grid emissions factors
Only decorative landscaping area
Number of ceiling fans in the office wing
Filter MERV ratings exclusively

explanation: Emissions comparisons require consistent units and marginal or average grid factors as appropriate.

distractorExplanations.b: Landscaping is unrelated.
distractorExplanations.c: Ceiling fans do not define DG emissions.
distractorExplanations.d: Filters are IAQ, not DG emissions.

prompt: Ultrasonic testing of steam traps helps identify:

options.text: Traps that are blowing live steam or blocked by distinguishing acoustic signatures
Chiller refrigerant charge levels
PV inverter firmware versions only
Roof membrane thickness exclusively

explanation: Non-invasive acoustic methods support steam system maintenance programs.

distractorExplanations.b: Refrigerant is unrelated to steam traps.
distractorExplanations.c: Inverter firmware is PV maintenance.
distractorExplanations.d: Roof membrane is envelope scope.

prompt: Boiler blowdown rate should be controlled to:

options.text: Maintain acceptable water chemistry while minimizing energy loss
Maximize blowdown continuously without measurement
Eliminate all dissolved solids monitoring
Ignore conductivity or TDS limits

explanation: Automatic blowdown on conductivity balances water quality and heat loss.

distractorExplanations.b: Excessive blowdown wastes energy.
distractorExplanations.c: Chemistry monitoring is required.
distractorExplanations.d: TDS/conductivity guides blowdown.

prompt: A heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) in cogeneration:

options.text: Produces steam from exhaust heat of a gas turbine or reciprocating engine
Cools refrigerant in a DX air conditioner
Stores electrical energy in batteries only
Replaces all combustion air requirements without a burner

explanation: HRSG captures exhaust energy for process or building steam loads in CHP systems.

distractorExplanations.b: HRSG is thermal recovery, not DX cooling.
distractorExplanations.c: Batteries store electricity, not exhaust heat.
distractorExplanations.d: Burners may still be needed for supplemental firing.

prompt: In industrial facilities, absorption chillers versus electric chillers trade off:

options.text: Heat input versus electrical kW/ton depending on relative fuel and power costs
Only initial paint color of the vessel
Elimination of all cooling towers always
Identical energy use regardless of heat source

explanation: Absorption uses thermal energy; economics depend on available waste heat or gas prices vs electricity.

distractorExplanations.b: Appearance is irrelevant to energy trade-offs.
distractorExplanations.c: Absorption systems may still use cooling towers.
distractorExplanations.d: Energy sources differ materially.

prompt: Combustion air preheat on industrial boilers uses:

options.text: Flue gas or process heat to raise incoming air temperature before the burner
Chilled water from the cooling tower exclusively
Return air from office VAV boxes only
No heat exchange with any stream

explanation: Preheated combustion air reduces fuel required to reach flame temperature.

distractorExplanations.b: Tower water is not typical combustion preheat source.
distractorExplanations.c: Office return air is not standard boiler preheat.
distractorExplanations.d: Preheat requires a heat source.

prompt: Optimal solar PV tilt and orientation for a fixed array in the northern hemisphere generally:

options.text: Faces south (or north in southern hemisphere) at tilt near latitude for annual energy
Faces straight down toward the ground always
Ignores shading from nearby structures entirely
Uses vertical east-only orientation for peak summer only without analysis

explanation: Site-specific modeling accounts for latitude, tariff, and shading for optimal yield.

distractorExplanations.b: Downward orientation captures minimal sun.
distractorExplanations.c: Shading dramatically reduces output.
distractorExplanations.d: Orientation should match load and resource goals.

prompt: Battery storage paired with DG or renewables can provide:

options.text: Peak shaving, backup power, and arbitrage where economics and controls support it
Infinite duration backup without sizing
Elimination of all interconnection protection
Guaranteed elimination of all demand charges without analysis

explanation: Storage value depends on tariff, duty cycle, and technology costs.

distractorExplanations.b: Duration is limited by capacity.
distractorExplanations.c: Protection remains required.
distractorExplanations.d: Demand charge savings vary by rate and usage.

prompt: Industrial energy intensity metrics (e.g., BTU per unit production) help:

options.text: Benchmark process efficiency and track improvements over time
Replace all safety regulations for boilers
Eliminate need for utility bills
Ignore production volume changes when comparing years

explanation: Normalizing energy by output reveals true efficiency trends.

distractorExplanations.b: Safety rules remain mandatory.
distractorExplanations.c: Utility bills still provide cost data.
distractorExplanations.d: Production volume must be included in normalization.

prompt: Package boilers versus field-erected units are typically chosen based on:

options.text: Capacity, pressure requirements, site access, and lifecycle cost
Interior carpet selection only
Number of BACnet points in the BAS
Color of stack paint exclusively

explanation: Smaller capacities often suit package units; large high-pressure plants may need field-erected designs.

distractorExplanations.b: Carpet is unrelated.
distractorExplanations.c: BAS points do not drive boiler type selection.
distractorExplanations.d: Stack paint is not a selection criterion.

prompt: A renewable portfolio standard (RPS) typically requires:

options.text: Utilities or suppliers to procure a percentage of energy from renewable sources
All buildings to install solar PV regardless of site conditions
Elimination of all natural gas use immediately nationwide
Boiler operators to ignore emissions permits

explanation: RPS policies drive renewable market demand through compliance obligations.

distractorExplanations.b: RPS applies to supply portfolios, not every building mandatorily.
distractorExplanations.c: Gas phase-out is policy-specific and gradual.
distractorExplanations.d: Emissions permits still apply.

prompt: Steam trap maintenance programs in industrial plants prioritize:

options.text: Regular inspection and replacement because failed traps waste fuel and can damage piping
Ignoring traps until catastrophic pipe failure only
Removing all traps to eliminate condensate
Operating with traps bypassed open continuously

explanation: Effective trap management reduces live steam loss, improves safety, and supports boiler efficiency.

distractorExplanations.b: Waiting for failures increases cost and risk.
distractorExplanations.c: Traps are required to remove condensate while blocking steam.
distractorExplanations.d: Bypassed traps pass steam continuously.
